name: Perceived Usefulness
definition: The degree to which a person believes that using a particular system would enhance his or her job performance.
sourcePaper: Davis (1989) - Perceived Usefulness, Perceived Ease of Use, and User Acceptance of Information Technology
measurementItems: Using the system improves my performance in my job.
Using the system increases my productivity.
Using the system enhances my effectiveness.
I find the system useful in my job.
notes: Core construct in technology acceptance. Often mediates the effect of PEOU on intention.

name: Perceived Ease of Use
definition: The degree to which a person believes that using a particular system would be free of effort.
sourcePaper: Davis (1989) - Perceived Usefulness, Perceived Ease of Use, and User Acceptance of Information Technology
measurementItems: Learning to operate the system is easy for me.
I find it easy to get the system to do what I want.
My interaction with the system is clear and understandable.
I find the system flexible to interact with.
notes: Often has both direct and indirect (via PU) effects on behavioral intention.

name: Behavioral Intention
definition: A person's subjective probability that he or she will perform a specified behavior.
sourcePaper: Fishbein & Ajzen (1975) - Belief, Attitude, Intention, and Behavior
theoreticalDomain: Theory of Reasoned Action / Planned Behavior
measurementItems: I intend to use the system in the future.
I will try to use the system in my daily work.
I plan to continue using the system.
notes: Key predictor of actual behavior in TRA/TPB. Mediates attitudes toward behavior. MPCU studies often predict usage directly; many extensions use intention as a proximal outcome.

name: Confirmation
definition: The extent to which the user's experience with the information system confirms his or her pre-acceptance expectations.
sourcePaper: Bhattacherjee (2001) - Understanding Information Systems Continuance
measurementItems: My experience with using the system was better than I expected.
The service level provided by the system was better than I expected.
Overall, most of my expectations from using the system were confirmed.
notes: Central to post-adoption research. Disconfirmation (negative confirmation) drives satisfaction changes.

name: Satisfaction
definition: The affective state reflecting the degree to which the user is pleased with the prior use of the information system.
sourcePaper: Oliver (1980) - A Cognitive Model of the Antecedents and Consequences of Satisfaction
measurementItems: I am satisfied with the performance of the system.
I am pleased with the experience of using the system.
I am contented with the use of the system.
notes: Key mediator between confirmation and continuance intention in ECM.

name: Continuance Intention
definition: The intention of a user to continue using an information system following initial adoption.
sourcePaper: Bhattacherjee (2001) - Understanding Information Systems Continuance
measurementItems: I intend to continue using the system rather than discontinue.
My intentions are to continue using the system.
I will keep using the system as regularly as I do now.
notes: Post-adoption equivalent of behavioral intention. Different from adoption intention.

name: Expectations
definition: Pre-consumption beliefs about product or service performance.
sourcePaper: Oliver (1980) - A Cognitive Model of the Antecedents and Consequences of Satisfaction
measurementItems: Pre-exposure expectation items adapted to context
notes: Forms baseline for confirmation/disconfirmation.

name: Perceived Performance
definition: The user's perception of how the system actually performed.
sourcePaper: Oliver (1980) - Expectation-Confirmation Theory
measurementItems: Performance perception items adapted to IS context
notes: Compared against expectations to form confirmation.

name: Actual Usage
definition: The actual behavior of using the information system.
sourcePaper: Davis (1989) - TAM
measurementItems: Self-reported or system-captured usage metrics
notes: Outcome of behavioral intention in adoption models.

name: Prior Expectations
definition: What users believed the technology would do for them before they started using it. A cognitive pre-use state that becomes the benchmark against which actual experience is judged.
sourcePaper: Bhattacherjee (2001) - ECM-IS; Oliver (1980) - ECT
measurementItems: Before using this product, I expected it to improve my farming decisions.
notes: In agritech, a farmer might expect a weather app to help them decide when to plant.

name: Continued Use
definition: The actual sustained usage of the technology over time. Often proxied by Continuance Intention in studies; can be measured via usage logs or follow-up surveys.
sourcePaper: Bhattacherjee (2001) - ECM-IS
measurementItems: Longitudinal usage data
Follow-up behavioural measures
notes: In Sub-Saharan Africa, gap between intention and behaviour may be influenced by infrastructural barriers (connectivity, cost, literacy).

name: Task Characteristics
definition: The attributes and requirements of the tasks that users need to perform. Includes task complexity, variety, interdependence, and the information and processing demands the task places on the user.
sourcePaper: Goodhue & Thompson (1995) - Task-Technology Fit and Individual Performance
measurementItems: Task complexity, variety, and interdependence items adapted to context
notes: One of two antecedents of fit; describes what the task demands from the user and the technology.

name: Technology Characteristics
definition: The capabilities, features, and constraints of the technology. What the system can do, how it presents information, and how well it supports the user's work.
sourcePaper: Goodhue & Thompson (1995) - Task-Technology Fit and Individual Performance
measurementItems: Technology capability and functionality items adapted to context
notes: One of two antecedents of fit; describes what the technology provides.

name: Task-Technology Fit
definition: The degree to which technology functionality matches the requirements of the tasks the user performs. The fit itself is measurable: when task demands align with technology capabilities, fit is high.
sourcePaper: Goodhue & Thompson (1995) - Task-Technology Fit and Individual Performance
measurementItems: The extent to which the technology meets my task needs
The technology has the right features for my tasks
The technology fits the way I work
notes: Central construct in Goodhue & Thompson’s Technology-to-Performance Chain (TPC). Mediates the effect of task and technology on utilization and performance.

name: Utilization
definition: The extent to which the user actually uses the technology to perform their tasks. Behavioural use of the system; can be voluntary or mandated.
sourcePaper: Goodhue & Thompson (1995) - Task-Technology Fit and Individual Performance
measurementItems: Frequency and extent of use
Use of specific features for task completion
notes: When fit is high, utilization is more likely; utilization can mediate the effect of fit on performance. In social cognitive theory applications (e.g. Compeau & Higgins), utilization is a key behavioral outcome influenced by self-efficacy and outcome expectations.

name: Performance Impact
definition: The effect of technology use on individual or organizational performance outcomes. The extent to which using the technology leads to better task completion, productivity, or effectiveness.
sourcePaper: Goodhue & Thompson (1995) - Task-Technology Fit and Individual Performance
measurementItems: Using the system improves my job performance
The system helps me accomplish tasks more quickly
The system has a positive impact on my work outcomes
notes: The key outcome in the TPC: technology is more likely to improve performance when fit is high and utilization occurs.

name: Personal Innovativeness
definition: The willingness of an individual to try out any new information technology.
sourcePaper: Agarwal & Prasad (1998) - A Conceptual and Operational Definition of Personal Innovativeness in the Domain of Information Technology
measurementItems: If I heard about a new information technology, I would look for ways to experiment with it.
Among my peers, I am usually the first to try out new information technologies.
I like to experiment with new information technologies.
notes: Often used as a moderator or antecedent in technology acceptance; reflects trait-based openness to trying new IT.

name: Privacy Concern
definition: The degree to which individuals worry about misuse of their personal data.
sourcePaper: Various – widely used in privacy and information systems research
measurementItems: I am concerned that my personal information could be misused.
I worry about how my data is collected and used by online services.
I am concerned about the privacy of my personal information.
notes: Commonly used in research on digital platforms, social media, health apps, and fintech.

name: Perceived Vulnerability
definition: How likely someone believes they are to experience harm (e.g., data breaches).
sourcePaper: Risk perception and protection motivation literature
measurementItems: I believe I am likely to experience a data breach or security incident.
I feel vulnerable to online threats when using this technology.
I think my personal information is at risk when I use digital services.
notes: Often appears in cybersecurity and privacy research.

name: Trust
definition: The belief that a system or organization will behave reliably and ethically.
sourcePaper: Various – foundational in IS, marketing, and organizational behavior
measurementItems: I believe this system/organization is trustworthy.
I feel confident that my interests will be protected.
I can rely on this system/organization to act with integrity.
notes: One of the most widely studied constructs in digital adoption research.

name: Hedonic Motivation
definition: Using a system because it is fun or pleasurable, not purely functional.
sourcePaper: Venkatesh et al. (2012) - UTAUT2
measurementItems: Using the system is fun.
I find the system enjoyable to use.
Using the system provides me with a lot of enjoyment.
notes: Very common in technology acceptance research; key in UTAUT2 for consumer contexts.

name: Perceived Enjoyment
definition: The extent to which using a technology is enjoyable in itself.
sourcePaper: Davis et al. – extended TAM; consumer and IS research
measurementItems: I find using the system enjoyable.
Using the system is pleasurable.
I have fun using the system.
notes: Closely related to hedonic motivation; often used in adoption of entertainment or experiential technologies.

name: Self-Efficacy
definition: A person's belief in their ability to successfully perform a task.
sourcePaper: Bandura - Social cognitive theory
measurementItems: I am confident I can use the system to complete my tasks.
I have the skills necessary to use the system effectively.
I am able to use the system even if there is no one to help me.
notes: Originates from Bandura's social cognitive theory; in tech research often measured as "computer self-efficacy" or domain-specific self-efficacy.

name: User Involvement
definition: The degree to which users participate in the development and design of a product.
sourcePaper: Various – innovation and information systems research
measurementItems: Users were actively involved in the product development process.
User feedback significantly influenced product decisions.
We collaborated with users when defining product requirements.
notes: Central to research on product development, system design, participatory innovation; example areas: co-creation, participatory design, open innovation. Often tested: User involvement → product innovation performance.

name: Market Uncertainty
definition: The extent to which customer needs, preferences, and market demand are unpredictable.
sourcePaper: Innovation and strategic management literature
measurementItems: Customer needs are difficult to predict.
The market for this product changes rapidly.
It is difficult to forecast demand.
notes: Classic construct in innovation and strategy. Common sources: changing user needs, emerging markets, lack of market data. Typical relationships: Market uncertainty → need for experimentation; Market uncertainty → product development performance.

name: Innovation Performance
definition: The extent to which an organization successfully develops and introduces new products or innovations.
sourcePaper: Innovation management research
measurementItems: Our new products outperform competitors.
Our innovation projects achieve their objectives.
Our organization successfully introduces new products.
notes: Widely used in innovation management. Typical dimensions: success of new products, novelty of innovations, commercial performance. Relationships often studied: Organizational capabilities → innovation performance.

name: Organizational Learning
definition: The process through which organizations acquire, interpret, and apply knowledge to improve performance.
sourcePaper: Organizational theory and strategic management
measurementItems: Our organization learns from past development projects.
Lessons from previous projects improve future work.
Knowledge is systematically shared across teams.
notes: Key aspects: learning from experimentation, knowledge acquisition, knowledge sharing. Typical relationships: Experimentation → organizational learning; Organizational learning → innovation performance.

name: Experimentation Capability
definition: An organization's ability to test ideas, generate evidence, and learn through structured experimentation.
sourcePaper: Modern innovation and digital product research
measurementItems: Our organization systematically tests new ideas.
Experiments are conducted before full implementation.
We use prototypes to evaluate product concepts.
notes: Example activities: A/B testing, prototyping, pilot trials. Typical relationship: Experimentation capability → product innovation success.

name: Technology Adoption Intention
definition: The likelihood that an individual plans to use a specific technology.
sourcePaper: Various – UTAUT and adoption models
measurementItems: I intend to use this technology in the future.
I plan to use this system regularly.
I expect to continue using this technology.
notes: Appears in many adoption models including UTAUT. Typical relationship: Perceived usefulness → adoption intention.

name: Social Influence
definition: The process by which individuals or groups affect the beliefs, attitudes, intentions, or behaviors of others. In research it is often operationalized as perceived social influence or subjective norms and measured using scales or experiments.
sourcePaper: Venkatesh et al. (2003) - UTAUT; Ajzen (1991) - Theory of Planned Behavior; Festinger (1954) - Social Comparison Theory; Kelman (1958) - Compliance, Identification, and Internalization
theoreticalDomain: Social psychology, technology adoption, marketing and consumer behavior
measurementItems: People who are important to me think I should use this system.
People who influence my behavior think I should use this system.
Friends and colleagues encourage me to use this technology.
notes: Key construct in UTAUT (one of four core determinants of technology acceptance). In TPB operationalized as subjective norms. Explains how peers, superiors, or cultural norms shape adoption decisions. Used in health behavior (e.g. vaccine uptake, smoking cessation) and social media research (e.g. likes, shares, influencer endorsements). Bridges micro-level and macro-level analyses.

name: System Quality
definition: The quality of the technical performance of a system.
sourcePaper: DeLone & McLean IS success model; information systems research
measurementItems: The system is reliable.
The system responds quickly.
The system functions consistently.
notes: Dimensions often include: reliability, speed, usability. Typical relationship: System quality → user satisfaction.

name: Information Quality
definition: The quality of the information produced by a system.
sourcePaper: DeLone & McLean IS success model; information systems research
measurementItems: The system provides accurate information.
The information is relevant to my tasks.
The information provided is complete.
notes: Common dimensions: accuracy, relevance, completeness. Typical relationships: Information quality → system usefulness; Information quality → user satisfaction.

name: Attitude
definition: A person's overall evaluation of performing a behavior—favorable or unfavorable—based on beliefs about its outcomes and their evaluated importance (expectancy–value). In TRA/TPB, attitude toward the behavior is a key antecedent of behavioral intention.
sourcePaper: Ajzen, I. (1991). The theory of planned behavior. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 50(2), 179–211.
theoreticalDomain: Theory of Reasoned Action / Planned Behavior
measurementItems: For me, performing this behavior would be good/bad.
For me, performing this behavior would be harmful/beneficial.
For me, performing this behavior would be unpleasant/pleasant.
notes: Formed from salient beliefs about the behavior; central to predicting intention alongside subjective norms and perceived behavioral control (TPB).

name: Subjective Norms
definition: The perceived social pressure to perform or not perform a behavior—whether important referents believe one should or should not engage in the behavior.
sourcePaper: Ajzen, I. (1991). The theory of planned behavior. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 50(2), 179–211.
theoreticalDomain: Theory of Reasoned Action / Planned Behavior
measurementItems: Most people who are important to me would approve of my performing this behavior.
People whose opinions I value would want me to perform this behavior.
It is expected of me that I perform this behavior.
notes: In UTAUT, a related idea appears as social influence. In TPB, subjective norm is one of three direct predictors of intention.

name: Perceived Behavioral Control
definition: The perceived ease or difficulty of performing a behavior, reflecting beliefs about control over internal and external factors that may facilitate or impede performance. Often linked to self-efficacy and controllability.
sourcePaper: Ajzen, I. (1991). The theory of planned behavior. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 50(2), 179–211.
measurementItems: For me, performing this behavior would be easy/difficult.
Whether I perform this behavior is entirely up to me.
I have the resources and opportunities needed to perform this behavior.
notes: Extends TRA to TPB; together with intention can predict behavior when volitional control is realistic. Distinct from but related to Bandura’s self-efficacy.

name: Facilitating Conditions
definition: The degree to which an individual believes that organizational and technical infrastructure exists to support use of the system. It captures enabling conditions and resources in the environment—such as device availability, connectivity, help-desk support, training, and compatibility with existing systems—rather than beliefs about the technology alone.
sourcePaper: Venkatesh, V., Morris, M. G., Davis, G. B., & Davis, F. D. (2003). User acceptance of information technology: Toward a unified view. MIS Quarterly, 27(3), 425–478.
measurementItems: I have the resources necessary to use the system.
A specific person (or group) is available for assistance with system difficulties.
I have the knowledge required to use the system.
The system is compatible with other systems I use.
measurementScale: Likert scale (adapted from Venkatesh et al., 2003)
notes: Type: enabling conditions / resources. Level: environment or context. In UTAUT, facilitating conditions (and sometimes intention) predict use behavior. Includes availability of resources, technical support, training, and compatibility with existing systems. At a higher level of analysis, the TOE environment context helps explain where such constraints and supports originate (e.g. infrastructure, institutions)—a bridge to individual-level FC in agritech and similar settings.

name: Voluntariness
definition: The degree to which use of a system is perceived as voluntary rather than mandated—reflecting freedom of choice and the absence of coercion or compulsory requirement to adopt.
sourcePaper: Venkatesh, V., Morris, M. G., Davis, G. B., & Davis, F. D. (2003). User acceptance of information technology: Toward a unified view. MIS Quarterly, 27(3), 425–478.
measurementItems: My use of the system is voluntary.
My manager does not require me to use the system.
Although it might be helpful, using the system is certainly not compulsory in my job.
measurementScale: Likert scale (context-dependent; stronger in mandatory settings)
notes: Type: contextual perception. Level: situational (mandatory vs voluntary adoption). Role in UTAUT: often modeled as a moderator—e.g. strengthening or weakening effects of social influence and expectancy constructs on intention when use is more or less voluntary.

name: Habit
definition: The extent to which people tend to perform behaviors automatically due to learning.
sourcePaper: Venkatesh et al. (2012) - UTAUT2
measurementItems: The use of the system has become a habit for me.
I am addicted to using the system.
I must use the system.
notes: In UTAUT2, habit captures automaticity and routinization of use; often predicts behavioral intention and use, especially in voluntary consumer contexts.

name: Price Value
definition: The consumer's cognitive trade-off between the perceived benefits of using a technology and the monetary cost of using it.
sourcePaper: Venkatesh et al. (2012) - UTAUT2
measurementItems: Using the system is reasonably priced.
At the current price, the system provides good value.
The benefits of using the system are worth the monetary cost.
notes: In UTAUT2, price value is typically a direct antecedent of behavioral intention, especially in voluntary consumer settings.

name: Perceived Benefit
definition: The extent to which users believe that adopting or using a technology will yield positive outcomes for them. In sub-Saharan African agritech contexts, perceived benefit is not limited to productivity or yield—it may include income improvement, better access to markets, reduced production or commercial risk, time savings, and greater resilience to shocks.
sourcePaper: Context-specific extension of outcome-expectancy and usefulness notions; widely used in agritech, ICT4D, and adoption studies in smallholder settings.
theoreticalDomain: Technology adoption; agritech and development information systems
measurementItems: Using this technology would improve my income or livelihood.
Using this technology would help me reach buyers or markets.
Using this technology would reduce risks I face in farming or selling.
Using this technology would save me time.
Using this technology would make my farming more resilient to setbacks.
measurementScale: 7-point Likert (Strongly disagree - Strongly agree); adapt wording to local pilot instruments.
notes: Align with Perceived Usefulness where performance is narrowly job-related; PB is broader and especially salient when benefits are economic, relational (markets), temporal, and risk-related—not only output per hectare.

name: Service Quality
definition: The quality of the support services surrounding an information system—such as responsiveness of help desks, training, and ongoing assistance—rather than the technical system or the information content alone.
sourcePaper: DeLone & McLean (2003) - Information Systems Success Model update
measurementItems: Support staff respond to my requests in a timely manner.
I receive adequate training to use the system.
When I need help, the support I get is of high quality.
notes: Added in the 2003 DeLone & McLean update alongside system and information quality as a driver of use, intention to use, and user satisfaction.

name: Intention to Use
definition: The strength of a user's intention to use an information system in the future; in success models it sits between system/information/service quality perceptions and outcomes such as satisfaction and net benefits.
sourcePaper: DeLone & McLean (2003) - Information Systems Success Model update
measurementItems: I intend to use the system in the next months.
I predict I would use the system.
I plan to use the system.
notes: Closely related to behavioural intention in TAM/UTAUT; ISSM positions ITU between quality perceptions and satisfaction/use.

name: Net Benefits
definition: The net positive impacts of an information system for individuals or organizations—aggregating and balancing benefits and costs. In the updated DeLone & McLean model, net benefits are the ultimate success criterion, subsuming earlier separate impact dimensions.
sourcePaper: DeLone & McLean (2003) - Information Systems Success Model update
measurementItems: The system has improved my effectiveness at work.
The benefits of the system outweigh its costs for my organization.
Overall, the system has a positive impact on how we operate.
measurementScale: Likert scale; organizational studies may use archival or financial measures.
notes: Success at the net-benefits level often requires prior success on quality, use, and satisfaction; measures should match the unit of analysis (individual vs organizational).

name: Technology Context
definition: In the Technology–Organization–Environment (TOE) framework, the technology context refers to characteristics of the innovation or system itself—such as relative advantage, compatibility, complexity, trialability, and observability—that influence organizational adoption and assimilation.
sourcePaper: Tornatzky & Fleischer (1990); widely applied in organizational IS adoption research (e.g. Zhu, Kraemer & Xu, 2006)
measurementItems: The technology offers clear advantages over our current way of working.
The technology is compatible with our existing systems and practices.
We can try the technology on a limited basis before full commitment.
measurementScale: Likert scale; items adapted to innovation and setting
notes: One of three TOE contexts; often combined with organizational and environmental factors in meta-models of innovation adoption.

name: Organization Context
definition: In the TOE framework, the organization context captures firm-level attributes that shape adoption—such as size, slack resources, managerial and technical capability, centralization, and formalization—that affect readiness to absorb and use new technology.
sourcePaper: Tornatzky & Fleischer (1990); applied in Zhu, Kraemer & Xu (2006) and many IS diffusion studies
measurementItems: Our organization has adequate financial resources to invest in this technology.
We have the technical skills internally to implement and use the technology.
Top management supports adoption of new information technologies.
measurementScale: Likert scale; organizational surveys or archival proxies (e.g. firm size)
notes: Contrasts with individual-level beliefs in TAM/ECM: TOE is typically measured at firm or unit level.

name: Environment Context
definition: In the TOE framework, the environment context refers to external forces—regulatory pressure, competitive intensity, partner readiness, infrastructure quality, and institutional support—that influence whether organizations adopt and sustain technology use.
sourcePaper: Tornatzky & Fleischer (1990); Zhu, Kraemer & Xu (2006)
measurementItems: Competitive pressure in our industry encourages us to adopt new technology.
Government policy and regulation support (or require) use of this type of system.
Infrastructure (e.g. connectivity, utilities) in our environment is adequate for adoption.
measurementScale: Likert scale; sector and country context strongly shape wording
notes: Often linked conceptually to individual-level facilitating conditions: macro environment shapes the resources and constraints users experience on the ground (e.g. in agritech in Sub-Saharan Africa).

name: Organizational Adoption
definition: The extent to which an organization decides to adopt, implement, and use a technology—in TOE studies often measured as adoption intention, assimilation stage, breadth of use, or similar firm-level outcomes rather than a single user’s continuance intention.
sourcePaper: TOE and innovation diffusion literature (e.g. Zhu, Kraemer & Xu, 2006)
measurementItems: Our organization has formally adopted this technology.
The technology is widely used across relevant units in our organization.
We intend to deepen use of this technology over the next year.
measurementScale: Likert scale or staged assimilation measures
notes: Unit of analysis is the organization; differs from individual-level continuance intention (CI) in ECM-style thesis models, though both can be narratively connected via context and facilitating conditions.

name: Outcome Expectations Performance
definition: Beliefs about the performance-related consequences of using a technology—such as improvements in job effectiveness, productivity, or quality of work—if one were to use it.
sourcePaper: Compeau & Higgins (1995); Compeau et al. (1999) — application of social cognitive theory to computer utilization
theoreticalDomain: Social cognitive theory; information systems use
measurementItems: Using computers in my job would enable me to accomplish tasks more quickly.
Using computers would improve the quality of the work I do.
Using computers would increase my productivity.
measurementScale: Likert scale (adapt wording to context; items stem from Compeau & Higgins)
notes: One of two outcome-expectations dimensions in Compeau & Higgins (1995); distinguished from personal outcomes (e.g. status, self-worth).

name: Outcome Expectations Personal
definition: Beliefs about the self-relevant or personal consequences of using a technology—such as recognition, image, or sense of competence—rather than task performance alone.
sourcePaper: Compeau & Higgins (1995); Compeau et al. (1999)
theoreticalDomain: Social cognitive theory; information systems use
measurementItems: Using computers in my job would give me greater control over my work.
Using computers in my job would improve my professional image.
Using computers would make my job more interesting.
notes: Paired with performance outcome expectations in IS applications of SCT; both influence intentions and use alongside self-efficacy and affect.

name: Affect
definition: The emotional response associated with performing or contemplating use of a technology—often positive or negative feelings toward computing in context (distinct from satisfaction with a specific episode of use).
sourcePaper: Compeau & Higgins (1995); affect in social cognitive accounts of computer use
theoreticalDomain: Social cognitive theory; affect and technology use
measurementItems: I feel comfortable using computers in my work.
I feel relaxed when using computers for my job.
I enjoy using computers for my work.
measurementScale: Likert scale; reverse or supplement items if measuring negative affect separately
notes: In Compeau & Higgins, affect toward computing relates to self-efficacy and usage; do not conflate with ECM satisfaction unless theory justifies it.

name: Anxiety
definition: Apprehension or unease about using computers or information technology—often termed computer anxiety in MIS research; tends to reduce confidence and use.
sourcePaper: Compeau & Higgins (1995); Compeau et al. (1999)
theoreticalDomain: Social cognitive theory; MIS and HCI
measurementItems: I feel apprehensive about using computers.
I hesitate to use computers for fear of making mistakes.
Using computers makes me nervous.
notes: Often negatively related to self-efficacy and utilization in SCT-based IS models; items should match the target technology.

name: Relative Advantage
definition: The degree to which an innovation is perceived as better than the idea, practice, or product it supersedes—often in terms of economic profitability, social prestige, or other valued outcomes.
sourcePaper: Rogers (2003) — Diffusion of Innovations; Moore & Benbasat (1991) for IS operationalizations
measurementItems: Using [innovation] improves the quality of the work I do.
Using [innovation] gives me greater control over my work.
Using [innovation] increases the effectiveness of my work.
measurementScale: Likert scale (adapt items to context; many IS studies adapt Moore & Benbasat–style wording)
notes: One of Rogers’ five perceived attributes of innovations. Frequently appears in TOE’s technology context and in individual-level adoption research.

name: Image
definition: The degree to which use of an innovation is perceived to enhance one’s status or standing in one’s social system—how much adopting or using the innovation improves how others see the user.
sourcePaper: Rogers (2003); Venkatesh & Davis (2000) — TAM2 social influence antecedent to perceived usefulness
theoreticalDomain: Diffusion of innovations; social influence in IS
measurementItems: People in my organization who use [technology] have more prestige than those who do not.
People who use [technology] have a higher profile in the organization.
Having [technology] is a status symbol among my peers.
measurementScale: 7-point Likert (Strongly disagree - Strongly agree); align wording with target system
notes: In Rogers, image is tied to the social visibility of adoption. In TAM2, Image is a social-influence path to perceived usefulness (distinct from subjective norm).

name: Observability
definition: The degree to which the results of an innovation are visible to others—how readily potential adopters can see evidence of the innovation’s effects (sometimes discussed informally as visibility of outcomes).
sourcePaper: Rogers (2003) — Diffusion of Innovations
measurementItems: I have seen others achieve clear benefits from using [innovation].
The positive results of using [innovation] are easy for others to notice.
It is easy for me to observe whether [innovation] works well for others.
measurementScale: Likert scale (Moore & Benbasat–style items are common in IS)
notes: Rogers’ fifth perceived attribute (alongside relative advantage, compatibility, complexity, and trialability). Often discussed as how visible the innovation’s results are to others. Do not confuse with organizational “visibility” of a project unless theory defines it that way.

name: Compatibility
definition: The degree to which an innovation is perceived as consistent with existing values, past experiences, and needs of potential adopters—fit with current practices, norms, and infrastructure.
sourcePaper: Rogers (2003); Moore & Benbasat (1991) — perceived characteristics in IS
measurementItems: Using [innovation] is compatible with how I prefer to work.
Using [innovation] fits well with my existing skills and habits.
[Innovation] works well with the systems and processes I already use.
notes: Central in Rogers’ attribute set and in TOE’s technology context (fit with organization and environment as well as user-level consistency).

name: Result Demonstrability
definition: The degree to which the outcomes or benefits of using a system are tangible, observable, and communicable to others—how easy it is to show or explain the positive results of use.
sourcePaper: Venkatesh & Davis (2000) — TAM2; conceptually related to observability in diffusion research
measurementItems: I would have no difficulty explaining to others the benefits of using [system].
The benefits of using [system] are clear to me.
I would have no difficulty showing others that using [system] leads to good outcomes.
notes: In TAM2, result demonstrability is a cognitive-instrumental antecedent of perceived usefulness. It overlaps conceptually with Rogers’ observability but is defined at the individual user’s ability to demonstrate outcomes of use.

name: Job Fit
definition: The degree to which an individual believes that using a computer or information technology on the job would enhance their performance of job-related tasks—compatibility between the technology and the demands of the work role.
sourcePaper: Thompson, Higgins & Howell (1991) — Model of PC Utilization; Triandis (1977) theory of interpersonal behavior
measurementItems: Using a PC makes it easier to do my job.
A PC is useful for the kinds of tasks I must perform in my job.
Using a PC improves my job performance.
measurementScale: Likert scale (adapt items to target technology and setting)
notes: Central cognitive belief in MPCU; closely related to task–technology fit and usefulness but defined as job-role fit in the Thompson et al. stream.

name: Complexity
definition: The degree to which an individual perceives that using a computer or information technology is difficult to understand and use—perceived difficulty and effort required to operate the system in the work context.
sourcePaper: Thompson, Higgins & Howell (1991) — Model of PC Utilization
measurementItems: Learning to use a PC for my job is easy for me.
My interaction with a PC is clear and understandable.
I find it easy to get a PC to do what I want it to do.
measurementScale: Likert scale (reverse-code if using ease-style items; align with study’s scoring)
notes: In MPCU this is perceived complexity of using the PC on the job—not Rogers’ innovation attribute at the organizational level. Often negatively related to use or intention.

name: Long Term Consequences
definition: Beliefs about the longer-run outcomes of using a computer or information technology for one’s job or career—effects that unfold over time rather than immediate task payoffs (e.g. skill development, career prospects, future flexibility).
sourcePaper: Thompson, Higgins & Howell (1991) — Model of PC Utilization; Triandis (1977)
measurementItems: Using a PC in my job will improve my long-term career prospects.
Using a PC helps me develop skills that will matter in the future.
Over time, using a PC will make me more effective in my role.
notes: Distinguish from short-term performance beliefs; in Triandis-style models it captures anticipated delayed outcomes of behavior.

name: Affect Towards Use
definition: The feelings of pleasure, enjoyment, or dislike associated with the act of using a computer or information technology in the job context—affective evaluation of use rather than purely instrumental beliefs.
sourcePaper: Thompson, Higgins & Howell (1991) — Model of PC Utilization; Triandis (1977)
measurementItems: I like using a PC for my job.
Using a PC for my work is pleasant.
I enjoy working with a PC.
notes: Labeled affect toward use in the MPCU stream; related but not identical to Compeau–Higgins affect or ECM satisfaction—keep definitions aligned to your measures.

name: Social Factors
definition: The degree to which an individual perceives that important others (e.g. supervisors, peers) believe they should use a computer or information technology, or the normative and referent influence of the social environment on use.
sourcePaper: Thompson, Higgins & Howell (1991) — Model of PC Utilization; Triandis (1977)
measurementItems: People who influence my work think I should use a PC.
My supervisor encourages me to use a PC for my job.
Most people whose opinions I value expect me to use a PC at work.
notes: Captures social/normative pressure in MPCU; overlaps with subjective norm in TRA/TPB but operationalization follows Thompson et al.

name: Intrinsic Motivation
definition: The degree to which an individual performs a behavior for its own sake—because the activity itself is interesting, enjoyable, or inherently satisfying—rather than to obtain separable consequences.
sourcePaper: Deci & Ryan (1985); Vallerand (1997) review of intrinsic and extrinsic motivation; Davis, Bagozzi & Warshaw (1992) in IS
measurementItems: I find using [technology] to be an enjoyable activity in its own right.
Using [technology] is fun for me.
I use [technology] because I want to, not only because I have to.
measurementScale: Likert scale (adapt to context; align with Davis et al. or self-determination–style wording)
notes: In Davis et al. (1992), intrinsic motivation to use computers in the workplace is a key antecedent of perceived usefulness and perceived enjoyment. Distinct from extrinsic motivation (instrumental or reward-driven reasons).

name: Extrinsic Motivation
definition: The degree to which an individual performs a behavior to obtain outcomes that are separable from the activity itself—such as rewards, performance gains, approval, or avoiding negative consequences.
sourcePaper: Deci & Ryan (1985); Vallerand (1997); Davis, Bagozzi & Warshaw (1992) in IS
measurementItems: I use [technology] because it helps me perform my job better.
I use [technology] because my work requires it.
Using [technology] helps me achieve outcomes that matter for my performance.
notes: In Davis et al. (1992), extrinsic motivation reflects instrumental reasons for use and predicts perceived usefulness and enjoyment alongside intrinsic motivation.
